nodes.label: brain py cli
nodes.description: brain.py — the click-based command-line entrypoint to the graph. Intentionally kept under 500 lines and free of semantic logic: every command is a thin wrapper that parses arguments, opens a Kuzu connection via lib.db.connect, dispatches to a function in lib/, and prints. The semantic logic lives in lib/. This split is why brain.py can be replaced or wrapped (global `brain` shim, the MCP server, the experiments) without touching ingestion/audit/query code.

nodes.label: click command group
nodes.description: The cli function is a click.group with a single shared option `--db PATH` and a context object carrying the db path. Every subcommand calls `_conn(ctx.obj['db_path'])` which falls back to DEFAULT_DB_PATH. This is how the global `brain` shim (point to a fixed brAIn install) and one-off `brain --db /tmp/test ingest ...` invocations coexist without separate code paths.

nodes.label: cli init command
nodes.description: `brain init` — opens (or creates) the DB and runs init_schema. Idempotent: safe to run on an existing DB. First command a fresh install needs.

nodes.label: cli ingest command
nodes.description: `brain ingest FILE.json` — loads the payload, calls lib.ingest.ingest_payload, then prints a one-block summary using the IngestReport fields (nodes_created/updated, rels_created/updated, purge counts, rejected_nodes count, rejected_rels count, rewritten_ids count, potential_duplicates count, skipped_rels count). Surfaces every audit signal in a single command so the user does not need a second tool to spot extraction issues.

nodes.label: cli find command
nodes.description: `brain find PATTERN` — case-insensitive substring search by id or label, default limit 30. Output: one line per match with id [type] label and (if non-empty) description. The primary tool for the human-mode anti-duplicate check before authoring a new payload.

nodes.label: cli show command
nodes.description: `brain show NODE_ID` — prints a node header (label, id, type, description, importance, sources) then two sections: ## Outgoing (each rel as `--[type c=NN f=...]--> dst (label)` plus the `« evidence »` lines) and ## Incoming. Exits 1 if the node does not exist.

nodes.label: cli causes command
nodes.description: `brain causes NODE [--depth 3]` — walks upstream via the CAUSAL_BACKWARD subset (causes/requires/enables/precedes), level by level. Prints each level distinctly so the user can read the causal chain top-down.

nodes.label: cli effects command
nodes.description: `brain effects NODE [--depth 3]` — walks downstream via the CAUSAL_FORWARD subset (causes/enables/precedes). Mirror of `brain causes`, level-by-level output.

nodes.label: cli paths command
nodes.description: `brain paths SRC DST [--max-hops 4] [--limit 10]` — enumerates paths between two nodes using Kuzu's variable-length pattern. Prints each path as a single arrow chain: `node1 --[type c=NN]--> node2 --[...]--> node3`. NOT restricted to causal types — any path explains connectivity.

nodes.label: cli context command
nodes.description: `brain context TOPIC` — wraps lib.query.context_for_topic. JSON output by default (pipes cleanly into an LLM prompt), `--no-json` for human reading. Designed to be the heuristic preflight: pass the topic of the document you are about to extract, dump the JSON into the system prompt, the LLM reuses existing ids.

nodes.label: cli query command
nodes.description: `brain query "<cypher>"` — raw escape hatch over lib.query.run_cypher. JSON output, empty result printed as `(empty result)`. Used for ad-hoc filtering the standard commands don't cover (e.g. all nodes added by a specific doc_id, all rels with low confidence).

nodes.label: cli stats command
nodes.description: `brain stats` — counts by node type and rel type, total node/rel counts. Cheap (two GROUP BY queries) — meant to be the first read after an ingest.

nodes.label: cli audit command
nodes.description: `brain audit [--json]` — runs lib.audit.run_audit, prints sections # Volumes, # Health (related_to ratio, no-description ratio, orphan ratio, single-source rels), # Avg confidence by rel type, # Top out-degree / in-degree, # Contributions by document, # Warnings, # Errors. Exits 0/1/2 from AuditReport.exit_code so CI can fail on errors.

nodes.label: cli export command
nodes.description: `brain export FILE` — wraps lib.export_import.write_export. Single JSON dump of the whole graph, intended for backup or transfer between machines.

nodes.label: cli import command
nodes.description: `brain import FILE [--strategy force|merge]` — wraps lib.export_import.read_import. Default strategy is `force` (wipe then load) so accidental imports do NOT silently mix two graphs. Note: the command is registered under the name 'import' (`@cli.command('import')`) because `import` is a reserved word in Python.

nodes.label: cli merge command
nodes.description: `brain merge SRC INTO DST` — explicit node merge. Syntax check: the second positional argument MUST be the literal word INTO (case-insensitive), otherwise click.UsageError. Designed for irreversible operations to read like English so the user double-checks direction (src disappears into dst, not the other way around).

nodes.label: into keyword safety
nodes.description: The required INTO keyword in `brain merge SRC INTO DST` is a deliberate ergonomic guard against irreversibility. Without it, a typo in argument order would silently merge the wrong direction. Cost: typing three extra characters; benefit: the command reads as English and refuses obviously wrong invocations like `brain merge SRC DST`.

nodes.label: cli pure plumbing design
nodes.description: Design rule for brain.py: no semantic logic above 'parse args / open connection / dispatch / print'. All branching that depends on graph content lives in lib/. Enables (a) MCP server reuse of the same lib functions, (b) UI backend reuse, (c) experiments scripts reuse, all without duplicating logic.

nodes.label: global brain shim
nodes.description: Optional ~/.local/bin/brain shell wrapper that calls `.venv/bin/python /path/to/brain.py "$@"`. Lets the user type `brain stats` from anywhere instead of `cd ~/Documents/brAIn && .venv/bin/python brain.py stats`. The README documents the cat-EOF recipe.

nodes.label: mcp server py
nodes.description: mcp_server.py — stdio MCP server exposing 8 graph tools to Claude. Registered globally in ~/.claude/settings.json under the `brain` server name so the tools are available in every Claude Code session regardless of the working directory. Uses the same lib/ functions as brain.py — the MCP layer is just the protocol adapter plus output formatters that mirror the CLI.

nodes.label: mcp eight tools
nodes.description: The 8 MCP tools registered by mcp_server.py: brain_find, brain_show, brain_causes, brain_effects, brain_paths, brain_stats, brain_query, brain_ingest. Each tool's description names the expected use (e.g. brain_find: 'Use this to check whether a concept already exists before creating a node'). The tool docstrings are part of the system prompt Claude sees — they are short cheat-sheets, not API docs.

nodes.label: mcp read-only default connection
nodes.description: `_conn(read_only=True)` is the default for the MCP server. Every tool except brain_ingest opens the DB in read-only mode — kuzu.Database(..., read_only=True). brain_ingest opens it writable. Rationale: lets multiple Claude sessions share the DB safely (Kuzu allows one writer + many readers); accidental destructive operations through query are impossible because the connection cannot write.

nodes.label: mcp one connection per call
nodes.description: Each MCP tool call opens a fresh kuzu.Database/Connection. No long-lived connection is held across calls. Cost: connection-open latency on every call (in practice negligible for an embedded DB). Benefit: stale-state issues vanish; concurrent writes from another process (e.g. a CLI ingest while the MCP is running) are picked up immediately.

nodes.label: mcp output format mirrors cli
nodes.description: The MCP formatters (_fmt_find, _fmt_show, _fmt_chain, _fmt_paths, _fmt_stats) intentionally reproduce the brain.py CLI output verbatim. Reason: Claude is trained-by-context on the CLI's textual conventions (`-- level 1 --`, `« evidence »` quotes, `--[type c=0.95]-->`); if the MCP returned different shapes the model would re-learn the structure poorly each session.

nodes.label: mcp exception swallow
nodes.description: call_tool wraps _dispatch in try/except and returns `Error: <message>` as a TextContent. Rationale: the MCP protocol does not gracefully recover from raised exceptions inside a tool call — Claude would see a transport error and the conversation might desync. Catching and stringifying preserves the conversational flow and gives Claude an actionable message it can interpret.

nodes.label: mcp stdio protocol
nodes.description: Server runs over mcp.server.stdio.stdio_server() — stdin/stdout JSON-RPC, no network. Matches how Claude Code spawns MCP servers: as child processes with piped stdio. No port, no auth, no network surface — security boundary is the file system.

nodes.label: mcp global registration
nodes.description: MCP server is registered in `~/.claude/settings.json` (user-level), NOT in a project-local file. Consequence: the 8 brain tools are available in EVERY Claude Code session, regardless of where the user is working. The path-portable design pays off here — a single registration covers all projects.

nodes.label: cli under 500 lines rule
nodes.description: Soft target for brain.py: stay under 500 lines. Enforces the pure-plumbing rule by exhaustion — once you cross 500 lines in brain.py, the new logic must go into lib/. Currently 388 lines, comfortable margin.

nodes.label: json output default for machine consumption
nodes.description: Commands aimed at piping into another tool (context, query, audit --json) default to JSON. Commands aimed at humans (show, causes, effects, paths, stats, audit) default to formatted text. The `--no-json` / `--json` flag flips the default where both modes make sense. Mixed defaults are intentional: one tool, two audiences.

rels.evidence: Implementation choice: cli is a click.group sharing --db across subcommands.
`brain init` is the user-facing wrapper around init_schema.
Calls lib.ingest.ingest_payload to perform the 4-phase upsert.
Provides the lookup primitive the interactive anti-dup protocol expects.
Calls lib.query.show_node and renders the result.
Exits with the report's exit_code so CI can gate on health.
Enforced via a UsageError check in the merge subcommand definition.
Requiring INTO catches wrong-direction merge invocations before they execute the irreversible delete.
The line cap is the structural enforcement of the pure-plumbing rule: when it would be exceeded, logic must move into lib.
A tiny shell wrapper turns brain.py into a globally-callable command without changing the underlying script.
Imports lib.db, lib.ingest, lib.query and reuses their functions verbatim.
TOOLS list in mcp_server.py defines all 8 tools.
Read-only connections let multiple Claude sessions share the DB and prevent destructive operations through brain_query.
The _fmt_* helpers reproduce brain.py's textual conventions.
call_tool wraps _dispatch in try/except to keep the conversational flow on errors.
Stdio is what makes the MCP server a zero-network attack surface — security boundary is the filesystem.
Registering in ~/.claude/settings.json makes the 8 tools session-wide and project-wide.
Global registration is only safe because brAIn has no working-directory dependencies.
Mixed-defaults convention applied across the CLI.
Two interfaces over the same lib/ — different protocols (CLI args vs MCP tool calls), same semantic operations.
Prints the IngestReport fields as a one-block summary.
brain_ingest tool formats its response from IngestReport.
